intro:
John (“the LORD is gracious”), son of Zebedee and Salome and brother of James, was a Galilean fisherman who became one of the Twelve Apostles. Working the nets on the Sea of Galilee, he and James left their father and boat at Jesus’ call (Matthew 4:21-22); their fiery zeal earned them the surname Boanerges, “sons of thunder” (Mark 3:17). He is traditionally identified as “the disciple whom Jesus loved,” the eyewitness behind the Fourth Gospel.

With Peter and his brother James, John belonged to the inner circle admitted to moments closed to the rest: the raising of Jairus’ daughter, the Transfiguration (Mark 9:2), and the agony in Gethsemane (Mark 14:33). At the Last Supper he reclined next to Jesus (John 13:23); he followed to the cross, where the dying Lord entrusted his mother Mary to him: “Behold, your mother” (John 19:26-27). On Easter morning he outran Peter to the empty tomb and “saw and believed” (John 20:2-8), and recognized the risen Lord by the shore of Galilee (John 21:7).

In the early church John stood beside Peter as a leader of the Jerusalem community. Together they healed the lame man at the temple gate and testified boldly before the Sanhedrin (Acts 3:1-8; Acts 4:13), and were sent to confirm the Samaritan believers (Acts 8:14). Paul names him with James and Cephas as one of the “pillars” of the church (Galatians 2:9). Early tradition places his later ministry at Ephesus and his exile to the island of Patmos “on account of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus” (Revelation 1:9).

John’s enduring legacy is his witness in writing — the Gospel of John, the three Johannine epistles, and the Revelation. His Gospel reflects most deeply on Christ’s divine identity, opening with “In the beginning was the Word” (John 1:1) and stating its aim: “that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name” (John 20:31). His letters press the marks of true faith — walking in the light, love for the brethren, and confessing the Son: “God is love” (1 John 4:8). Several other men named John appear in the New Testament, notably John the Baptist and John Mark, but the apostle remains the great theologian of the Word made flesh.
hitchcock_meaning: the grace or mercy of the Lord
